blurb: A tidy chartreuse dome that blazes red-orange in autumn.
seasons: Sulfur-yellow bracts spring; green summer; red/orange/purple fall
toxic: Milky sap irritates skin/eyes — wear gloves; caution with kids/pets

care.hardiness: USDA zones 4–8 — fully winter-hardy on the Boulder Front Range (zone 5b–6a). The mound dies back to the crown in winter and re-emerges reliably each spring. No protection needed beyond good drainage; sitting in wet soil through freeze-thaw cycles is the one thing that kills it.
care.sun: Full sun is ideal — 6+ hours gives the tightest, brightest dome. It tolerates light afternoon shade, but too much shade causes the plant to lose its characteristic cushion shape and become open and floppy.
care.soil: Thrives in lean, dry to medium, sharply drained soil — tolerates poor, rocky, sandy, or clay ground as long as it doesn't stay wet. The alkaline clay-loam soils common on the Front Range suit it well without amendment. Rich, heavily amended, or constantly moist soil leads to lax growth and dulls fall color.
care.water: Low and drought-tolerant once established — it gets by on Front Range rainfall (~15–18 in/year) with only supplemental watering during extended dry spells. Water deeply but infrequently through the growing season; avoid overhead irrigation that keeps foliage wet, and ease off entirely in late fall to help harden the crown before freeze-up.
care.spacing: Space 12–18 in apart; each plant matures to roughly 12–18 in tall and as wide (occasionally wider in rich soil). The compact dome habit means it rarely needs adjustment — set it and leave it.
care.bloom: Showy sulfur-yellow bracts appear in April–May on the Front Range, typically 3–4 weeks of peak color. The lime-chartreuse display is long-lasting even after the true flowers fade. Foliage turns vivid red-orange-purple in autumn for a second season of interest.
care.feeding: None needed on average to poor soils — lean conditions keep the mound tight and bright. A light balanced fertilizer in early spring is fine on genuinely poor ground, but skip it on typical Front Range soils; excess nutrients produce loose, soft growth and muted fall color.
care.maintenance: Cut spent flowering shoots back to the base in late summer or fall once color fades — this keeps the mound tidy and prevents unwanted self-seeding. Wear gloves (and eye protection when pruning): the milky white latex sap is a significant skin and mucous-membrane irritant. Do not compost prunings carelessly — wash tools afterward. Divide clumps in early spring every 4–5 years if the center begins to open up; take basal cuttings in spring, dipping cut ends in water to stop sap flow before potting.
care.selfsow: Can self-seed modestly; deadhead promptly after bloom if you want to control spread. Seedlings are easy to spot and pull when small.
care.troubles: Remarkably trouble-free — no serious pests or diseases on the Front Range. The one vulnerability is crown rot from poorly drained or chronically wet soil; ensure good drainage, especially over winter. Sap causes contact dermatitis in sensitive people and is harmful to eyes — keep kids and pets away from cut stems.
care.companions: Pairs beautifully with late-spring bloomers that share its lean, well-drained conditions: blue flax, bearded iris, wallflower, catmint, and ornamental alliums. The bright spring bracts contrast well with blue-purple tones. Fall foliage color reads well next to little bluestem grass.
